summary: Signing/crypto/identity/provider modules are well-architected with strong OBP compliance and thorough test coverage. After 7 rounds of review (coder v1-v2, codeanalyzer v1-v2, tester v1-v2, security v1), the code is solid. Found 1 minor cross-file contract gap, 2 nits — no critical or major issues. All three previous reviewers did quality work; no significant gaps between them.

previous_reviews_assessed.codeanalyzer: agree — thorough 5-pass analysis caught real issues (IKeyProvider return type, bare catch, duplication). v2 verification was correct.
previous_reviews_assessed.tester: agree — coverage gap analysis was accurate, 28 new tests meaningfully improved quality. Minor: didn't flag the IdentityData error-swallowing contract.
previous_reviews_assessed.security: agree — crypto verification is sound, threat model alignment is correct. Medium findings are genuine but acceptable in user-sovereign model.

findings.issue: IdentityData.ResolveDefault() silently swallows all errors — returns null on provider failure, DataSource failure, or key generation failure. Callers (Actor.Identity → DynamicData("MyIdentity") → MemoryStack) get null without knowing why. The DynamicData lambda on Actor.cs:79 evaluates engine.System.Identity.Value which hits this path. If the DataSource is corrupted or the key provider fails, %MyIdentity% silently becomes null with no diagnostic trail.
findings.impact: Silent null for %MyIdentity% in production when DataSource has issues. User gets no error message — just null variables in their PLang program. Low blast radius (only affects first identity resolution per engine), but hard to debug.
findings.suggestion: Consider logging (or storing on IdentityData.Error) the failure reason so that callers can inspect why resolution failed. Not a code change — a design note for when the logging/diagnostics layer is added.

findings.issue: SignedData.CreateAsync uses action.Context.MemoryStack.GetValue("NowUtc") and casts to DateTimeOffset without null check. Same pattern in DefaultIdentityProvider.GenerateIdentity (line 285). Both assume NowUtc is always registered — true today, but fragile if someone constructs a bare MemoryStack without registering system variables.
findings.impact: NullReferenceException if NowUtc is not registered. Currently impossible in normal execution (MemoryStack constructor registers it), but possible in isolated test scenarios or if someone creates a context without the standard setup.
findings.suggestion: Acceptable as-is — system variables are always registered by MemoryStack constructor. Document the invariant if not already documented.

findings.issue: ToSigningBytes() mutates Signature to null then restores it (save-mutate-restore pattern). This is technically not thread-safe, but PLang's single-threaded execution model makes it safe in practice. Security bot already flagged this as Low (#4).
findings.impact: None in current architecture. Would become a real bug if SignedData instances were ever shared across async contexts. Already documented by security review.
findings.suggestion: No action needed — already documented. If PLang adds concurrent execution, this should be revisited.
